prompt:
scanfeed.py works, but nobody can extend it. It's continuation-passing all the way down — process_scan_lines takes three callbacks, and inside it there's a parse function that takes a callback, calling a QA filter that calls a dedupe closure that finally calls your on_scan. Adding one stage last month meant threading a new closure through four levels of nesting, and worse: the whole feed has to be driven to completion — the live dashboard only wants the first N surviving scans of a tailing feed, and today it can't stop early.

Rewrite it as a generator pipeline. Target API, same module: iter_records(lines, bad=None) lazily yields parsed, normalized record dicts (blank/comment lines skipped; malformed lines reported to the optional bad(lineno, line, reason) callable and skipped); drop_qa(records, dropped=None) and dedupe(records, duped=None) are lazy generator stages with optional observer ports for what they discard; summarize(records) is the consuming sink returning the events/packages/delivered summary; run(lines, bad=None) wires the whole thing together and returns the same shift-summary dict the callback version produced, bad_lines/duplicates/qa_dropped counts included. The callback entry points (process_scan_lines, process_scan_file) are dead once this lands — callers are migrating to run(), and file handling is the caller's problem since any iterable of lines works.

Big fat constraint: BEHAVIOR DOES NOT CHANGE. Same parsing, same normalization, same 'last kept scan per package' dedupe rule, same reason strings, same summary numbers. And the stages must be genuinely lazy — one record out, one line in, no buffering; the dashboard tail depends on it. test_scanfeed.py is the contract (python3 test_scanfeed.py) and it checks laziness with an instrumented source, so no list() shortcuts. The test file is off limits.
